does: This paper develops RAGLens, a lightweight hallucination detector for retrieval-augmented generation that identifies unfaithful outputs by analyzing internal representations of large language models. The method employs sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose LLM activations into interpretable features, then uses information-theoretic feature selection to identify features that specifically activate during hallucination events. The detector operates through additive feature modeling, combining selected features to predict whether generated text contradicts or extends beyond retrieved source documents, achieving accurate hallucination detection without requiring large-scale training data or external LLM judges.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability that study how neural network activations encode semantic information. It extends prior research on hallucination detection in RAG systems, which has traditionally relied on either expensive supervised detectors requiring annotated data or costly external LLM judges. The use of sparse autoencoders directly addresses limitations in prior interpretability methods that attempted to leverage internal representations for hallucination detection but achieved limited accuracy. The paper combines interpretability techniques with RAG-specific challenges, responding to the open problem of ensuring factuality in retrieval-grounded generation without incurring substantial computational or annotation costs.
enables: By demonstrating that SAE-based feature extraction can isolate hallucination signals in LLM activations, this work opens directions for deploying efficient, in-context hallucination detection without external dependencies. The interpretable features identified could enable deeper understanding of what triggers factuality failures in RAG, potentially informing training procedures that reduce hallucinations. The lightweight detector design enables practical deployment in resource-constrained settings and could extend to other faithfulness and safety problems beyond hallucination detection. Future work might leverage discovered features for post-hoc correction of unfaithful generations or for improving LLM training to better ground outputs in provided evidence.
